volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: Amphitryon (Section 3.2.937c - Section 4.3.1035)

sections.label: Section 3.2.937c
sections.original: Bene facis .
sections.translation: You act properly . For in the life of mortals many things of this nature come to pass ; and now they take their pleasures , again they meet with hardships .

sections.label: Section 3.2.940
sections.original: irae interveniunt , redeunt rursum in gratiam .
sections.translation: Quarrels intervene , again do they become reconciled . But it perchance any quarrels of this nature happen between them , when again they have become reconciled , twofold more loving are they between themselves than they were before .

sections.label: Section 3.2.944
sections.original: Primum cavisse oportuit ne diceres ,
sections.translation: At the first you ought to have been careful not to say so ;

sections.label: Section 3.2.945
sections.original: verum eadem si isdem purgas mi , patiunda sunt .
sections.translation: but if you excuse yourself to me for the same , it must be put up with .

sections.label: Section 3.2.946
sections.original: Iube véro vasa pura adornari mihi ,
sections.translation: But bid the sacred vessels to be got ready for me , that I may fulfil all those vows which I made when with the army , in case I should return safe home .

sections.label: Section 3.2.949
sections.original: Ego istuc curabo .
sections.translation: I ’ ll take care of that .

sections.label: Section 3.2.949b
sections.original: Evocate huc Sosiam ;
sections.translation: Call out Sosia hither .

sections.label: Section 3.2.950
sections.original: gubernatorem qui in mea navi fuit
sections.translation: Let him fetch Blepharo , the pilot that was on board my ship , to breakfast with us . ( Aside .) He shall be fooled this day [*] so as to go without his breakfast , while I shall drag Amphitryon hence by the throat .

sections.label: Section 3.2.954
sections.original: Mirum quid solus secum secreto ille agat .
sections.translation: It ’ s surprising what he can be arranging alone in secrecy with himself . But the door opens ; Sosia ’ s coming out .

sections.label: Section 3.3.956
sections.original: Ámphitruo , assum . sí quid opus est , impera , imperium éxequar .
sections.translation: Amphitryon , I ’ m here ; if any way you have need of me , command me ; your commands I will obey .

sections.label: Section 3.3.957
sections.original: Sosia , optume advenis .
sections.translation: Very opportunely are you come .

sections.label: Section 3.3.957b
sections.original: Iam pax est inter vos duos ?
sections.translation: Has peace been made then between you two ? But since I see you in good humour , I ’ m delighted , and it is a pleasure to myself . And so does it seem becoming for a trusty servant to conduct himself ;

sections.label: Section 3.3.960
sections.original: proinde eri ut sint , ipse item sit ; voltum e voltu comparet :
sections.translation: just as his superiors are , so should he be likewise ; by their countenances he should fashion his own countenance ; if his superiors are grave , let him be grave ; if they rejoice , let him be merry . But come , answer me ; have you two now come to a reconciliation ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.963
sections.original: Derides , qui scis haec dudum me dixisse per iocum .
sections.translation: You are laughing at me , who know full well that these things were just now said by me in joke .

sections.label: Section 3.3.964
sections.original: An id ioco dixisti ? équidem serio ac vero ratus .
sections.translation: In joke did you say it ? For my part , I supposed that it was said seriously and in truthfulness .

sections.label: Section 3.3.965
sections.original: Habui expurigationem ; facta pax est .
sections.translation: Still , I ’ ve made my excuses ; and peace has been made .

sections.label: Section 3.3.965b
sections.original: Optume est .
sections.translation: ’ Tis very good .

sections.label: Section 3.3.966
sections.original: Ego rem divinam intus faciam , vota quae sunt .
sections.translation: I shall now perform the sacrifice in-doors , and the vows which I have made .

sections.label: Section 3.3.966b
sections.original: Censeo .
sections.translation: So I suppose .

sections.label: Section 3.3.967
sections.original: Tu gubernatorem a navi huc evoca verbis meis
sections.translation: Do you invite hither , in my name , Blepharo , the pilot , from the ship , so that when the sacrifice has been performed , he may breakfast with me .

sections.label: Section 3.3.969
sections.original: Iam hic ero , cum illic censebis esse me .—
sections.translation: I shall be here again , while you ’ ll be thinking that I ’ m there .

sections.label: Section 3.3.969b
sections.original: Actutum huc redi .
sections.translation: Return here directly .

sections.label: Section 3.3.970
sections.original: Numquid vis , quin abeam iam intro , ut apparentur quibus opust ?
sections.translation: Do you wish for anything else , but that I should go in-doors now , that the things that are requisite may be got ready ?

sections.label: Section 3.3.971
sections.original: I sane , et quantum potest parata fac sint omnia .
sections.translation: Go then , and take care that everything is prepared as soon as possible .

sections.label: Section 3.3.972
sections.original: Quin venis quando vis intro ? faxo haud quicquam sit morae .—
sections.translation: Why , come in-doors whenever you please ; I ’ ll take care that there shall not be any delay .

sections.label: Section 3.3.973
sections.original: Recte loquere et proinde diligentem ut uxorem decet .
sections.translation: You say well , and just as befits an attentive wife . ( ALCMENA goes into the house .) Now both of these , both servant and mistress , are , the pair of them , deceived ,

sections.label: Section 3.3.975
sections.original: qui me Amphitruonem rentur esse : errant probe .
sections.translation: in taking me to be Amphitryon ; egregiously do they err . Now , you immortal Sosia , take you care and be at hand for me . You hear what I say , although you are not present here . Take care that you contrive to drive away Amphitryon , on his arrival just now , by some means or other , from the house .

sections.label: Section 3.3.980
sections.original: volo déludi illunc , dum cum hac usuraria
sections.translation: I wish him to be cajoled , while with this borrowed wife I now indulge myself . Please , take care that this is attended to just in such way as you know that I desire , and that you assist me while to myself I am offering sacrifice [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.4.984
sections.original: Concédite atque abscédite omnes , dé via decédite ,
sections.translation: Stand by and make room all of you , get you out of the way .

sections.label: Section 3.4.985
sections.original: nec quísquam tam audax fúat homo , qui óbviam obsistát mihi .
sections.translation: And let not any person now be so presumptuous as to stand before me in the road . For surely , why , by my troth , should I , a God , be any less allowed to threaten the public , if it does not get out of my way , than a slave in Comedies [*] ? He is bringing news that the ship is safe , or else the approach of some angry old blade ; whereas I am obeying the bidding of Jove , and by his command do I now hie me .

sections.label: Section 3.4.990
sections.original: quam ob rem mihi magis par est via decedere et concedere .
sections.translation: For this reason , it is more fitting to get out of the road and to make room for me . My father calls me , I am following him , to his orders so given am I obedient . As it befits a son to be dutiful to his father , just so am I to my father ; in his amours I play second fiddle to him , I encourage him , assist him , advise him , rejoice with him . If anything is pleasing to my father , that pleasure is an extremely great one for myself .

sections.label: Section 3.4.995
sections.original: amat : sapit ; recte facit , animo quando obsequitur suo ,
sections.translation: Is he amorously disposed ? He is wise ; he does right , inasmuch as he follows his inclination ; a thing that all men ought to do , so long as it is done in a proper manner . Now , my father wishes Amphitryon to be cajoled ; I ’ ll take care , Spectators , that he shall be rarely cajoled , while you look on . I ’ ll place a chaplet on my head , and pretend that I am drunk .

sections.label: Section 3.4.1000
sections.original: atque illuc sursum escendero : inde optume aspellam virum
sections.translation: And up there ( pointing to the top of the house ) will I get ; from that spot , at the top of the house , I ’ ll cleverly drive this person off when he comes hither : I ’ ll take care that , sober , he shall be drenched . Afterwards , his own servant Sosia will presently be suffering the punishment for it ; he ’ ll be accusing him of doing , this day , the things which I myself have done what ’ s that to me ? It ’ s proper for me to be obedient to my father ; it ’ s right to be subservient to his pleasure .

sections.label: Section 3.4.1005
sections.original: sed eccum Amphitruonem , advenit ; iam ille hic deludetur probe ,
sections.translation: But see ! here is Amphitryon ; he ’ s coming . Now shall he be rarely fooled , if , indeed , ( to the AUDIENCE ) you are willing , by listening , to lend your attention . I ’ ll go in-doors , and assume a garb [*] that more becomes me ; then I ’ ll go up upon the roof , that I may drive him off from hence .

sections.label: Section 4.1.1009
sections.original: Naúcratem quem cónvenire vólui , in navi nón erat ,
sections.translation: Naucrates , whom I wanted to find , was not on board ship ;

sections.label: Section 4.1.1010
sections.original: neque domi neque in urbe invenio quemquam qui illum viderit .
sections.translation: neither at home nor in the city do I meet with any one that has seen him ; for through all the streets have I crawled , the wrestling-rings and the perfumers ’ shops , to the market , too , and in the shambles , the school for exercise , and the Forum , the doctors ’ shops , the barbers ’ shops , and among all the sacred buildings . I ’ m wearied out with seeking him , and yet I nowhere meet with Naucrates .

sections.label: Section 4.1.1015
sections.original: nunc domum ibo atque ex uxore hánc rem pergam exquirere ,
sections.translation: Now I ’ ll go home , and from my wife will I continue to make enquiry into this matter , who the person was , by the side of whom she submitted her body to dishonor . For it were better that I was dead , than that I this day should leave this enquiry incomplete . ( Goes up to the door .) But the house is closed . A pretty thing indeed ! This is done just like the other things have been done : I ’ ll knock at the door . ( Knocks .)

sections.label: Section 4.1.1020
sections.original: aperite hoc . heus , ecquis hic est ? ecquis hoc aperit ostium ?
sections.translation: Open this door ; ho there ! is there anybody here ? Is any one going to open this door ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1021
sections.original: Quís ad fores est ?
sections.translation: Who ’ s that at the door ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1021b
sections.original: Égo sum .
sections.translation: ’ Tis I .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1021c
sections.original: Quid ego sum .
sections.translation: Who ’ s ’ tis I ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1021d
sections.original: Íta loquor .
sections.translation: ’ Tis I that say so .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1021e
sections.original: Tibi Iúppiter
sections.translation: For sure , Jupiter and all the Deities are angered with you who are banging at the door this way .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1023
sections.original: Quo modo ?
sections.translation: In what manner ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1023b
sections.original: Eo modo , ut profecto vivas aetatem miser .
sections.translation: In this manner , that without a doubt you must be spending a wretched life .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1024
sections.original: Sosia .
sections.translation: Sosia .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1024b
sections.original: Ita , sum Sosia , nisi me esse oblitum existimas .
sections.translation: Well ; I ’ m Sosia , unless you think that I ’ ve forgotten myself .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1025
sections.original: quid nunc vis ?
sections.translation: What do you want now ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1025b
sections.original: Sceleste , at etiam quid velim , id tu me rogas ?
sections.translation: What , you rascal , and do you even ask me that , what it is I want ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1026
sections.original: Ita , rogo . paene effregisti , fatue , foribus cardines .
sections.translation: I do so ask you ; you blockhead , you ’ ve almost broken the hinges from off the door . Did you fancy that doors were supplied us at the public charge ? Why are you looking up at me , you stupid ? What do you want now for yourself , or what fellow are you ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1029
sections.original: Verbero , etiam quis ego sim me rogitas , ulmorum Acheruns ?
sections.translation: You whip-scoundrel , do you even ask me who I am , you hell of elm-saplings [*] ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1030
sections.original: quem pol ego hodie ob istaec dicta faciam ferventem flagris .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , this day I ’ ll make you burn with smarts of the scourge for these speeches of yours .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1031
sections.original: Prodigum te fuisse oportet olim in adulescentia .
sections.translation: You surely must have formerly been a spendthrift in your young days .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1032
sections.original: Quidum ?
sections.translation: How so ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1032b
sections.original: Quia senecta aetate á me mendicas malum .
sections.translation: Because in your old age you come begging a hap-ill [*] of me for yourself .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1033
sections.original: Cum cruciatu tuo istaec hodie , verna , verba funditas .
sections.translation: Slave ! for your own torture do you give vent to these expressions this day .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1034
sections.original: Sacrufico ego tibi .
sections.translation: Now I ’ m performing a sacrifice to you .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1034b
sections.original: Qui ?
sections.translation: How ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.1034c
sections.original: Quia enim te macto infortunio .
sections.translation: Why , because I devote you to ill-luck [*] with this libation .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1035
sections.original: Vós inter vos partite ; ego abeo , mihi negotium est ;
sections.translation: Do you settle these matters between yourselves ; I ’ m off , for I have business ; and I do not think that I have ever anywhere beheld such extraordinary wonders .
